title: Calamares a la Romana
subtitle: Spanish fried squid rings in crisp batter
excerpt: Calamares a la romana are rings of squid fried in a light, crisp batter until golden. They are one of the most popular tapas in Spain. The batter is minimal, the frying is quick, and the key is serving them immediately while they are still hot and crisp.
sourceNotes: Calamares a la romana are a staple of Spanish tapas bars. The name 'a la romana' refers to the style of batter-frying, which is common across Roman and Spanish cuisines. The dish is eaten all over Spain as a bar food and a light tapa.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The squid can be cleaned and sliced ahead. Batter and fry to order.

content.text: Calamares a la romana are squid rings dipped in a light batter and fried in hot olive oil until golden and crisp. The batter is very simple: flour, water or beer, and salt. The squid cooks in about 2 minutes; the difficulty is keeping the oil at the right temperature so the batter crisps before the squid toughens.

items.prepNote: cleaned, tubes sliced into 1 cm rings
or cold sparkling water or light beer

content.text: Pat the squid rings thoroughly dry with kitchen paper. Season with a little salt. Dry squid batters better and doesn't spit as much in the oil.

Make the batter: whisk the flour and salt together, then whisk in the cold wine or water gradually until you have a smooth, slightly thick batter. It should coat a spoon thinly. Keep it cold; cold batter makes a crisper coat.
Heat {{olive-oil}} in a deep pan to 180°C. To test without a thermometer, a drop of batter should sizzle and rise immediately.

Working in batches, dip the squid rings in the batter, let the excess drip off, and slide gently into the hot oil. Fry for 1 to 2 minutes until pale golden and crisp. Don't overcrowd the pan. Transfer to kitchen paper with a slotted spoon. Serve immediately with lemon wedges.

items.symptom: The squid is rubbery inside the crisp batter
items.cause: Overcooked, or squid not dried before battering
items.fix: Squid rings no thicker than 1 cm cook through in under 2 minutes. Remove them as soon as the batter is golden. Wet squid steams inside the batter rather than frying cleanly.

items.symptom: The batter falls off in the oil
items.cause: Oil not hot enough, or squid not dried
items.fix: The oil must be at 180°C. If it's too cool, the batter soaks off before it sets. Pat the squid very dry before dipping.

content.text: Calamares a la romana is found on tapas menus across Spain and is one of the most recognisable bar foods in the country. In Madrid, a calamares bocadillo (fried squid sandwich on a white roll) is a local street food classic. Serve the rings as a tapa with cold drinks and lemon alongside.
